1: <header>Life cycle assessment of milk production system in Brazil: Environmental impact reduction linked with anaerobic treatment of dairy manure </header>
Alyne Martins Maciel Marcelo Henrique Otenio a, b, , Vanessa Romário de Paula a, , Virgínia Mendonça Lourenço Benhami Cassiano Moro Piekarski Camila Marçal da Rocha d Nathan Oliveira Barros a

b Embrapa Gado de Leite, Juiz de Fora, State of Minas Gerais 36038-330, Brazil

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: LCA Cattle Waste management Anaerobic digestion Biogas|Earlier Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) analyses have shown that dairy manure treatment by anaerobic digestion reduces the environmental impacts; however, the magnitude of this reduction is currently unknown in Brazil. This paper aims to assess the environmental impacts of milk production farms (with and without anaerobic digester) in Brazil with LCA. Additionally, quantify the reduction of environmental impacts associated with the treatment of dairy manure through anaerobic digestion in three different product systems: (1) the Embrapa farm with anaerobic digestion (AD), (2) the Embrapa farm without anaerobic digestion (NAD1), and (3) the Colinas farm without anaerobic digestion (NAD2). The analysis was performed using software OpenLCA© assuming 1 kg of fat and protein-corrected milk as a functional unit. Our results show that anaerobic digestion (AD scenario) promotes biogas and digestate production, which besides providing clean energy and organic fertilizer pro- ductions, can help reduce on average 25 % and 38 % of the impacts on acidification, eutrophication, climate change, energy use, photochemical oxidation, and ozone depletion, compared to NAD1 and NAD2, respectively. Then, if Brazil applied anaerobic digester to treat animal manure and encourages biogas production, besides the extra clean energy production, a part of the actions to mitigate CO2 emission could be achieved.
2: Tropical farming has considerable importance in dairy production, with Brazil representing the fourth largest milk producer globally Embrapa - Empresa Brasileira de Pesquisa Agropecuária. [14]. The livestock and agriculture sectors and their environmental impacts are projected to expand alongside the world's population Goglio [20]. Milk production has a negative effect on natural resources, causing many other impacts, including eutrophication, acidification, photochemical oxidation, and ozone depletion Bacenetti [5], ,Wang [56]. In addition, if globally considered, livestock collaborates with climate change and is responsible for 14 % of anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions [19].
Anaerobic digestion expresses a very sustainable way of treating dairy cattle manureMassaro [36],Esteves [15]. The biogas generated

during anaerobic digestion is useful as renewable energy, which reduces the dairy farming environmental impact and mitigates GHG emissions [45], Sahota [50]. The digestate produced has a high concentration of nutrients as nitrogen, potassium, and phosphorus, and can be used as an organic fertilizer in harvests cultivation, avoiding the purchase and use of conventional fertilizers, improving soil quality, and cause socio- economic and environmental benefitsLijó [33,39,45].

A committee established by the Brazilian Low Carbon Agriculture Plan aims to reduce GHG emissions across cattle manure treatment by anaerobic digestion (ABC Plan, 2018). In countries like Brazil, anaerobic digestion has grown due to the tropical climate, and availability of extensive areas to perform anaerobic digestion with low technology and the use of manure as a raw material source (Cherubini et al., 2015a). Some points that could be critical for the adoption of anaerobic digestion are related to the region's climate, raw input availability, use of
transportation, plant location, and technological development level used Esteves [15].

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a methodology robust and commonly used to evaluate direct and indirect environmental impacts of goods, products, and services holistically Baldini [6]. LCA studies regarding dairy manure treatment by anaerobic digestion show that biogas pro- duction using manure as a raw material reduces total GHG emissions by up to 25 % compared to raw manure sludge in the USA[26]. The anaerobic digestion reduces 27.7 kg CO2 eq. per ton of manure, mainly due to the avoided emissions of replacing fossil fuels with biogas in Iran [52]. A study in Italy established that anaerobic digestion of manure reduced some environmental impacts: acidification in 29 %, global warming in 22 %, and eutrophication potential in 18 % Bacenetti [5].

This paper aims to evaluate the environmental impacts of milk production processes in Brazil, using the LCA method, investigating the improvements on the environmental performance linked with inte- grating anaerobic decision in the treatment of dairy manure to milk production farms. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first quan- titative comparison of six categories of environmental impacts (acidifi- cation, eutrophication, climate change, photochemical oxidation, and ozone depletion) in the Brazilian milk production chain, in a paper that quantifies the impact reduction caused by treating dairy cattle manure in the anaerobic digester. Besides that, we have not seen discussions about the effects caused by possible changes in the dairy cattle diets in their GHG enteric emissions, as analyzed in this study through sensi- tivity analysis.

6: To evaluate the potential environmental impacts in the milk pro- duction processes, the LCA was performed with OpenLCA © 1.6.3 Software, under ISO 14040:2006[30]and ISO 14044:2006[31 stan- dards, which describe the requirements and guidelines and the princi- ples and structure of the LCA, respectively. The Standard separates LCA into four phases, followed by this study.

Our study aimed to evaluate the environmental impacts of three study cases (two milk production farms), identifying the major envi- ronmental impacts and estimating the magnitude of their reduction by using the anaerobic digester. Two models were adopted for the first farm: AD, real conditions, and NAD1 simulating the removal of the anaerobic digester. The third case was the Colinas farm, NAD2, which operates without an anaerobic digester. Both farms are located in Brazil and operate with semi-intensive management systems.
7: <header>System boundaries and functional unit (FU) </header>

For both subsystems, boundaries are from "cradle-to-farm gate". Our inputs include the planting of maize, transport, diesel fuel, electricity, acquisition of materials to the farm, the use of materials needed to manage the herd, milking, milk cooling and storage, the generation, disposal, and treatment of dairy manure, and emissions from manure management. The gate is the delivery of milk to dairy transporters, going through all the processes until the production cycle is completed.

Using FU allows comparisons with other dairy farms that work with different breeds or use various types of feed for the herd (International Dairy Federation, 2015). We established the FU as one kilogram of fat and protein-corrected milk (FPCM), leaving the farm gate for this study. We calculated FPCM using the formula defined by the International Dairy Federation International Dairy Federation [29]:
where FPCM: F = fat (%) and TP = True protein (%). The main characteristics of the livestock farms studied are shown in Table 1.

Allocation procedures

The studied farms produce only milk in their production chain, being the meat and milk production independent systems, as usual in our country. For that reason, the potential environmental impacts evaluated are totally attributed to the milk system.

The AD model has co-products (electricity and organic fertilizers) due to the anaerobic digester use. Because of this scenario variation, a comparative LCA of three different systems for milk production evalu- ated the magnitude of the environmental impact reduction by using the anaerobic digester. The main functional processes of our systems are the production of milk and the production of biogas and organic fertilizer from the manure management system.

There are two ways of coping with the multi-functionality issue: using the portioning method or the avoided burdens (or substitution method)[55,34,25]; Cherubini et al., 2015a; Hanserud [24]. The hier- archy of solutions proposed by ISO 14044 recommends avoiding allo- cation (ISO 14044, 2006b; De Léis [12]. Thereby, in the substitution method, once we compare systems that fulfill the same functions (electricity and organic fertilizer, in this study), the allocation is not needed[55].

Although not referred to in the ISO14044 (ISO 14044, 2006b), several authors have indicated that the substitution method is theoret- ically comparable to system expansion[55,34,25]. They might not sup- ply identical results, but they yield compatible results when compared [25]. According to [34], there are two ways for expanding boundaries: adding the subsystem that provides the "missing" function to the studied subsystems; or subtracting the subsystem that provides the "excessive" function. In the latter, "avoided emissions" will happen, and environ- mental impact becomes negative.

Therefore, we use the substitution method in the AD scenario for the organic fertilizers and the electricity from biogas. For this, we have not considered electricity and organic fertilizers products in the allocation method; instead, we considered them as creditsBattini [7], (Cherubini et al., 2015a). Due to avoided emissions, we used these credits analogous to emissions related to electricity from the grid and inorganic fertilizers, including all processes relating to the amounts of these flowsBacenetti [5], Battini [7]; Cherubini et al., 2015a; Hanserud [24],Esteves [15].

11: In both studied farms, the processes and associated environmental impact of the milk production chain are mainly related to animal feed; use of energy; and treatment of manure and cattle enteric emissions.

Inventory data regarding the input used for milk production for both farms were collected through interviews with specialists and employees of the respective farms located in Coronel Pacheco, Minas Gerais. Each farm was visited several times, and average input values were collected during one year of production from 2017 to 2018. Primary data on the amount of milk produced, animals' diets, transport of inputs, fuel con- sumption, and related flows were obtained. We considered for transport an average distance between farms and factory producers, and lorry weight and type (unit in t.km), for all products or inputs purchased.
However, whenever it was unfeasible to use primary data, we used

background data or secondary data from Ecoinvent databases 3.3 (2016), scientific articles, and technical reports. For example, some studies as Amon [4] and Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change [28], were used for calculating the GHG emissions due to manure management and due to enteric fermentation. We used the Ecoinvent database for external inputs and outputs, such as producing inorganic fertilizers and pesticides, feed, diesel fuel, electricity, and activities off- farm-gate. The LCI of AD, NAD1and NAD2 is shown in the supplemen- tary material.

13: Cattle feed is composed of pasture, silage, and concentrate. The silage is produced within the farm since the crop production until the feed is ready to be eaten. The concentrate needs to be purchased else- where. Primary data information about the cropping system (land use, fertilizer, and herbicides), the occupation of land for pasture, the ma- terials purchased, and the transport of inputs were obtained within the farm.

The farms represent a semi-confined feedlot system with silage and pasture haw agricultural production, which is representative of dairy farms of the region. The Embrapa farm has a total area of agricultural production of 71 ha, whose pasture production occupies 85 % of the total area and the corn silage production 15 % of the total area. For the Colinas farm, the pasture area consists of 50 ha, and the corn silage production consists of 12 ha.
14: Some activities in the milk production chain as the pumping used in the dairy manure treatment system and the milking operations require electricity[38]. For the AD scenario, all the required energy demand is met by using biogas from the biodigester. In the NAD1 e NAD2 sce- narios, the Brazilian electricity mix was applied. Information about energy use was collected directly in the farms.
15: We used data from the literature to quantify the GHG emission of manure management and emissions due to enteric fermentation. The

environmental impacts of livestock farming are linked to CH4 and nitrous oxide (N20) emissions, resulting from manure management and enteric fermentation. CH4 production is associated with the amount and quality of food consumed and animals' diets, which are linked to age, weight, growth rate, and milk production Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change [28].

The IPCC Tier 2, Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change [28], was used to calculate GHG emissions, such as CH4 enteric emissions. This is the most recommended method to estimate GHG emissions Bal- dini [6]. The production of manure daily generated by dairy cattle corresponds to approximately 10 % of their body weight[ Based on that, approximately 45 to 48 kg/cow/day were used to calculate the waste produced on the studied farms.

Regarding the N2O and CH4 emissions from manure, both depend on the nitrogen and carbon content, storage time and type, treatment of the wastes, and subsequent application in the soil Amon [4], Intergovern- mental Panel on Climate Change [28]. We calculated the CH4 and N2O emissions from manure management using the study carried out by Amon [4], which evaluated emissions rates during the treatment, stor- age, and application of waste in the soil following anaerobic digestion and without treatment.

The Embrapa farm treatment system (Fig. 1), AD scenario, comprises a continuous anaerobic plug-flow digester, operated with hydraulic retention meantime of 32 days, responsible for treating residue gener- ated by dairy cattle. The digester is filled with washing water from the free stall floor, and subsequently, the water passes through the anaer- obic digestion process and proceeds to the stabilization pond. The water is pumped again from the pond to wash the free stall and reused on average for 20 to 25 days[38].

In the AD scenario, fertilizers are a combination of organic and inorganic parts. The organic part is obtained from the digestate of anaerobic digestion. Technical and scientific studies have been con- ducted to attest the viability of digestate application on crops Maciel [35] following the adjusted maintenance substitution principle Han- serud [24], where the correct amount of organic fertilizer needed is
Fig. 1. Flowchart of the Embrapa farm with anaerobic digester (AD).
adjusted with the crop requirement, and local soil characteristics. Be- sides that, by using the anaerobic digester, the Embrapa farm reduces water, fertilizers, and electricity.

The biogas obtained from anaerobic digestion is transformed into electrical energy sufficient to perform all farm activities that involve milk production, such as the pumping used in the dairy manure treat- ment system and the milking operations[3 The main characteristics of the biogas produced at the Embrapa farm (AD) are descript in Table 2.

Treatment data of wastewater from dairy cattle, such as the anaer- obic digester and the electricity-generator motor, was considered with an average life span of 20 years and converted to the FU. For AD sce- nario, the ICV of inputs, outputs, and emissions of the entire process was elaborated for the system.

For Embrapa farm, a second model was also carried out (Fig. 2), NAD1, in which we disregarded the dairy manure treatment to evaluate the effect of the anaerobic digestion. In this model, we eliminated the inputs and outputs by biogas and the digestate produced. The NAD1 applies animal waste in the soil only as a final disposal alternative to dairy manure. As no quantitative nutrient studies address the raw manure from NAD1, it is not possible to reduce the use of inorganic fertilizer. We converted, in the software, the fertilizer from organic to inorganic, and other inputs related to these flows were modified, such as increasing the values pertaining to packaging and the transportation of fertilizers. In addition, it was necessary to change the sources of the electric energy used at the farm.

19: The Colinas farm operates with a semi-intensive management system composed of pasture and food supply, such as concentrates and silage. The farm does not have an anaerobic digester for dairy manure treat- ment (Fig. 2). Instead, when confined, the waste from animals is removed by water and applied to the soil. However, given that there is no technical follow-up, no study has been conducted to obtain the cor- rect dosages for application to the crops. Therefore, we cannot consider this operation a substitute for inorganic fertilizers, being inorganic fer- tilizer the only one used on the NAD2.
20: From the primary data collected on the farms and secondary data obtained from the Ecoinvent® 3.3 databases, the LCI data from the Embrapa and the Colinas farms were inserted into the OpenLCA © 1.6.3 software.

We quantified the potential impacts according to CML 2001 (base- line) version 4.4[23]. The assessment method follows the problem- oriented by midpoint approach. It provides data on categories of impact assessment, such as climate change, acidification, eutrophica- tion, energy use, ozone depletion, and photochemical oxidation. The CML method is the most widely adopted in studies of milk LCA Baldini [6].
Uncertainty analysis is a test performed to quantify the uncertainty

Uncertainty analyses were performed with a data quality matrix (Pedigree Matrix) combined with the Monte Carlo statistical method [37]. The Pedigree Matrix is composed of five indicators of data quality. For each indicator, we assigned a score from 1 to 5, with 1 being the highest quality grade and 5 the worst Weidema, et al. [57]. The LCI input and output data were scored according to the uncertainty factors of the Matrix Pedigree, available in the OpenLCA software tool; the score assigned for each input and output data can be observed in the Sup- plementary material. The Monte Carlo simulation consists of a random sampling of the probability of each uncertain parameter. It is a very effective method to evaluate uncertainties, besides being one of the most recommended parameters for LCA studies [8,9].

We performed the data uncertainty analysis based on the Pedigree Matrix available in OpenLCA® using the log-normal distribution. The simulation was performed in the OpenLCA®, using the Monte Carlo method, and considering 10,000 independent simulations with a 95 % confidence interval. In the Monte Carlo simulation, a random variation of the input parameters occurs according to the uncertainty distribu- tions, and the final results obtained were exported into Excel format for analysis[1]. In addition, the OpenLCA software tool also provides the results of the qualitative assessment of the environmental impacts, with scores for each category evaluated. The data uncertainty analysis result can be observed in the Supplementary material.

24: Sensitivity analysis is a method used to evaluate the effect of selected methods and data on the results of a LCA study (NBR ISO 14040, 2004). Sensitivity analysis can estimate how a change in the model or data used may influence inventory, environmental profile, or any other LCA result [49]. Once it points to input parameters that leverage the results, we performed the sensitivity analysis for the hotspots identified in our study: cattle feed and cattle enteric GHG emission. According to the original IPCC calculation, cattle feed is composed of pasture, silage, and concentrate with a total digestibility of 60%. Once the concentrate is the most impacting food option, we performed the sensitivity analysis changing its constitution (corn grains, soybeans, and urea), including the variations observed in their transport. In a concentrate volume reduction scenario, the cows probably would eat more pasture to attend to the daily amount of food required, this change reduces the total food digestibility (to 60 % from %), and raise the enteric emission Grandl [22].

Therefore, we build four distinct scenarios for sensitivity analysis: scenario 1 (S1) % reduction of the concentrate consumption; scenario 2 (S2) % reduction of the concentrate consumption; scenario 3 (S3) 2 % reduction of the concentrate consumption combined with the di- gestibility of 55 %; scenario 4 (S4) 5 % reduction of the concentrate consumption combined with the digestibility of 55 %.

26: The AD scenario (with the biodigester) showed the best environ- mental performance for all analyzed impact categories compared to NAD1 and NAD2 (without the biodigester (Fig. 3). The results support the adoption of anaerobic digesters as a form to reduce the impacts of milk production, as has been suggested Bacenetti [5], Battini [7]. On average, AD scenarios had 25 % and 38 % lower impact than NAD1 and NAD2 scenarios, respectively, for the categories of climate change, acidification, eutrophication, energy use, ozone depletion, and photo- chemical oxidation (Fig. 3).
The reduction in the magnitude of the environmental impacts related
Fig. 2. Flowchart of the Embrapa farm without anaerobic digester (NAD1) and the Colinas farm (NAD2).
Fig. 3. The impacts of the scenarios with anaerobic digester (AD) and without anaerobic digester (NAD1 and NAD2). FPCM: fat and protein corrected milk.

with the use of the anaerobic digester is related to several reasons. The first one is biogas production, which can be employee as renewable energyLijó [33] and is a promising renewable energy source, with a growing potential due to its high market capacity Sahota [50], decreasing the need use of electricity from the grid. Beyond this, anaerobic digestion produces the digestate, which can be used as NPK fertilizers for crop production, once it contains essential nutrients for the plant. Although the anaerobic digester does not change the concentra- tion of the nutrients in the raw manure, this is an effective solution for

eliminating pathogens and reducing GHG emissions Sahota [50]. In addition, anaerobic digestion makes nutrients more available and accessible to crops, when compared to raw manure[2] Ammoniacal nitrogen is the most abundant; moreover, it contains a considerable concentration of potassium, calcium, phosphorus, and magnesium [39]. The digestate applied as fertilizer, reduced the use of the inorganic fertilizer, avoiding its associated impacts such as the emissions air and the use of inputs of production, including packaging and transportation. Moreover, it solves an environmental liability: the large volume of dairy
manure daily generated, which may create diverse and considerable environmental damage if not treated properly.

Comparing the scenarios which do not have the anaerobic digester (NAD1 and NAD2), NAD1 presented on average 16 % lower impact (Fig. 3). The difference can be explained by the high productivity of NAD1 compared to NAD2, which allows that the former requires lower amounts of transport, fertilizers, maize, and soybean, among other in- puts per FU, as shown in LCI. In this case, NAD2 presented the less sustainable scenario for all impact categories assessed. The reduction in acidification, eutrophication and climate change because of the increase in milk productivity has also been observed by other researchers[5,56].

Food production is the main responsible for the negative impacts in all scenarios (Figs. 4 and 5), including soybean, maize, and urea, alongside the upstream inputs, such as transports, silage production, and food preparation for the animals. The same as observed by Rafiee [47], which shows that the feed production stage dominates and promotes many adverse effects. In our study, food production contributes 60 % of the impacts (Fig. 4), considering all impact categories for all scenarios (AD, NAD1, and NAD2). Nguyen et al. [43] show that in agriculture, on average, 96 % of the environmental impacts are related to the animal feed production phase, considering the upstream and downstream pro- cesses involved. Agricultural production is essential to animal feed and highly depends on inputs such as land, fertilizers, fuel, machinery, pesticides, and electricity Silva [54], explaining the diverse forms of environmental impact associated with it.

The GHG emissions from cattle also had considerable influence in the impact categories, mainly in the climate change (77 %, 79 %, and 78 for AD, NAD1, and NAD2, respectively) and photochemical oxidation (37 %, 46 %, and 37 % for AD, NAD1, and NAD2, respectively) (Fig. 5). GHG emissions from cattle include enteric emissions from cow and manure management emissions. De Léis [12] also found that in the feedlot system for milk production, feeding carried out the most impacts in climate change, representing 47 % of the total GHG emission, fol- lowed by enteric fermentation, which is responsible for 36 %. The milking process also affects the use of energy and the cleaning and auxiliary materials used to operate the milking machine, milking parlor and, milk room [13]. In addition, as Fig. 5 shows, the main advantages in the AD scenario are avoiding the use of electricity from the grid and inorganic fertilizers. Thus, the AD scenario has positive impacts (bene- ficial, those shown below the red line in Fig. 5), which minimize the negative impacts of the total sum.

33: The deposition of many pollutants like sulfur dioxide (SO2), nitrogen oxides (NOx), ammonia (NH3), and N2O in the soil and water results in acidification. However, the presence of an anaerobic digester system reduces acidification by avoiding the emissions of these compounds from inorganic fertilizers[1]. The anaerobic digestion of manure pro- motes benefits such as the reduction of methane, nitrous oxide and ammonia emissions, as well as electricity production from renewable sources Baldini [6]. AD scenario reduces acidification potential by 21 % and 40° compared with NAD1 and NAD2, respectively. In accordance, Bacenetti [5] found that anaerobic digestion reduces 29 % the acidifi- cation potential. Inorganic fertilizers accounted for about 14 % of the acidification impacts on NAD2 and about 18% on NAD1. Along the milk production chain, food production is the main factor responsible for acidification for both models (Fig. 5).

The AD scenario presented a 21 % and 27 % lower impact on the climate change category comparing to NAD1 and NAD2, respectively. This is because of the anaerobic digestion that lowers CH4 emissions from dairy manure, converting it into biogas and carbon dioxide (CO2), which has lower global warming potential Neshat [42]. Using biogas as a renewable energy source can reduce the impacts, which is also in line with climate-friendly farming practices Neshat [42,46]. Other studies had shown the efficiency of the anaerobic digester in reducing GHG emissionsAmon [4],Battini [7],Famiglietti [16]. Using renewable en- ergy is a key to reduce GHG emissions, and biogas plays a remarkable role in this process (GIWA et al., 2017).

The enteric fermentation and manure management from livestock were identified as important hotspots concerning climate change, fol- lowed by food production for animal feed, including all upstream pro- cesses (Fig. 5). Our results align with previous papers that demonstrated the link between climatic change impact and enteric emissions, use, and production of fertilizer, manure management, food production and transportation, and low farm productivitySeó [53], Wang [56].

Eutrophication is primarily influenced by phosphorus and nitrogen dischargedLijó [33]. The presence of the anaerobic digester reduced eutrophication impact, AD had a 13 % and 40 lower impact potential than NAD1 and NAD2, respectively. The main reason for this result is the contrast between manure disposal and the manure management process Wang [56].

In the AD scenario, a large volume of dairy manure daily generated, which would otherwise be disposed of into the environment, is trans- formed into biogas and organic fertilizers. Hence, this reduction is probably an effect of the digestate's use as a nitrogen and phosphorus source, thereby avoiding the damage environmental due to the process of the inorganic fertilizers' manufacture and transportation, which should be acquired in the absence of the digestate. When using the organic fertilizer, credit is obtained to avoid the impacts and the emis- sions from the inorganic fertilizer Hanserud [24].

Food production, which considers upstream impacts such as input transport and fertilizer use, is mainly responsible for eutrophication in all scenarios (Figs. 4 and 5). During the cultivation stages of maize and soybean, nitrate and phosphate are released into the water and the ammonia is emitted into the air increasing the eutrophication[3].

When considering the impacts of the energy used, non-biological resources are considered, such as fossil fuels, minerals, and metals[1]. The absence of these resources lowers the life-cycle emissions for AD (39 % and 45 % lower than NAD1 and NAD2, respectively), as this scenario uses the electricity produced by the anaerobic digester. When
Fig. 5. Influence of each process for each impact category for scenarios with anaerobic digester (AD) and without anaerobic digester (NAD1 and NAD2).
renewable energy represents a significant component of reducing emissions, biogas comes to play a key role (Giwa et al., 2017).

The most striking flows for energy demand were food production, followed by milking (Fig. 5). Transportation and the diesel process also had a significant influence on this impact. Several studies were con- ducted to know about the environmental aspects of dairy farming, concluding that feed production is the major contributor to energy use and environmental pollution Wang [56]. In addition, agricultural ma- chinery operations handle the main share of the environmental load for energy use because diesel fuel consumption plays an important role Bacenetti [5].

The ozone depletion category refers to the reduction of the strato- spheric ozone layer due to anthropogenic emissions of substances such as chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), halons, and hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs)[1]. Processes such as the use of diesel in farm machinery and the transportation of inputs are primarily responsible for these emis- sions. In the dairy production chain, electricity use is also responsible for ozone depletion Famiglietti [16]. Additionally, ozone formation in- creases due to higher methane emissions[41].

The AD scenario had a 46 % and 48 % lower environmental impact than NAD1 and NAD2, respectively. This is attributed to the reduction of emissions from enteric fermentation and waste management and from transportation (Fig. 5). In addition, the use of energy produced by digester prevents relevant values of emissions Massaro [36]. In this study, the milking process includes electricity use, transport use, and many processes such as food production and fertilizers (for NAD1 and NAD2). For this reason, the food, transport, and milking processes had a more significant influence on the results. GHG emissions, transport, and the use of fertilizers are the most striking factors for ozone depletion in milk production González-García [21].

39: Photochemical oxidation is contingent on carbon monoxide (CO), sulfur dioxide (SO2), nitrogen oxide (NO), ammonium, and non-CH4 volatile organic compounds [1]. The critical factor for this impact in both scenarios was CH4 emission from enteric fermentation, and manure management, and food production for cattle (Fig. 5). Furthermore, this impact category is predominantly influenced by the volatile organic

compounds (VOCs) of NOx and CH4, mainly from the manure of dairy cows, including nitrogen oxides from the use of fossil fuel for food transportation, and CH4 from the enteric fermentation process [10].

The AD scenario presented almost 7 % and 29 % lower photo- chemical oxidation than NAD1 and NAD2, respectively. Photochemical oxidation was the impact in which the AD and NAD1 scenarios presented the slightest difference. In addition, biogas had negative interference in the AD scenario. The operation of anaerobic digester plants, with biogas production, generates pollutants like CO2, CH4, phosphate (PO and SO2 Esteves [15]. That may have influenced our results for the AD scenario.

Furthermore, Battini [7] assessing the anaerobic digestion in dairy farms to the production of electricity, found that its manner is envi- ronmentally friendly and reduces environmental damage, although local impacts may increase in the form of photochemical oxidation. The au- thors describe that the reduced emissions of CH4 from storage and the credits gained from substituted electricity cannot compensate for NOx emission growth in biogas engine combustion. This may explain the slight difference between the AD and NAD1 results.
Potential to reduce GHG emission in Brazil due to the installation of digesters in dairy farms

Our results showed that 24 % of emissions were reduced by anaer- obic digestion for each kg of milk. A farm that treats manure with anaerobic digestion issues 0.8835 kg of CO2 eq. per 1 kg of milk against 1.1622 kg of CO2 eq. per 1 kg of milk for a farm that does not treat its manure (mean between NAD1 and NAD2). Each year, 34.39 billion kg (35 billion liters) of milk are produced in Brazil (Embrapa, 2018). If all farms adopted an anaerobic digester to treat animal manure and facili- tated biogas production, 9.58x109 kg of CO2 eq. would not be emitted per year (considering that the farms still do not carry out the treatment of dairy manure and will do it through anaerobic digesters).

The Government of Brazil has noticed to the Secretariat of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) the intended Nationally Determined Contribution (iNDC) of reducing GHG emission by 43 % at 2030, concerning 2005 levels Contribution [27], representing the amount of 1.2x1012 kg of CO2 eq (iNDC, 2015). If the 9.58x10 kg of CO2 eq. that would not be emitted per year due to the anaerobic digester were considered, a part of the mitigation actions

The analysis presented was carried out only considering dairy farms in anaerobic digestion systems. Several other farms with different ani- mal's products, such as pigs and chicken's farms, also have the potential to use biomass for anaerobic digestion and subsequent electricity gen- eration. In addition, any other organic waste such as discarded food scraps also has the potential for this purpose[48]. If there were in- centives in anaerobic digestion use, the reduction in emissions could reach a relevant value. It is also important to note that the agriculture and electricity sectors are among the most significant GHG emitters in Brazil, representing 28 % and 19 %, respectively, of total Brazilian emissions[51]. Therefore, the transformation of waste into biogas and the use of biogas for energy production could contribute to minimizing GHG emissions.

Considering the magnitude of such integration is known that a consequential study could have been selected. Although, this present study does not have a focus on this future visualization, but assess sce- nario which already exists. Besides that, an attributional study is more suitable when there are no decisions to be made [18].

The Government of Brazil has noticed to the Secretariat of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) the intended Nationally Determined Contribution (iNDC) of reducing GHG emission by 43 % at 2030, concerning 2005 levels Contribution [27], representing the amount of 1.2x1012 kg of CO2 eq (iNDC, 2015). The analysis presented was carried out only considering dairy farms in anaerobic digestion systems. Several other farms with different ani- mal's products, such as pigs and chicken's farms, also have the potential to use biomass for anaerobic digestion and subsequent electricity gen- eration. In addition, any other organic waste such as discarded food scraps also has the potential for this purpose[48]. If there were in- centives in anaerobic digestion use, the reduction in emissions could reach a relevant value. It is also important to note that the agriculture and electricity sectors are among the most significant GHG emitters in Brazil, representing 28 % and 19 %, respectively, of total Brazilian emissions[51]. Therefore, the transformation of waste into biogas and the use of biogas for energy production could contribute to minimizing GHG emissions.

Considering the magnitude of such integration is known that a consequential study could have been selected. Although, this present study does not have a focus on this future visualization, but assess sce- nario which already exists. Besides that, an attributional study is more suitable when there are no decisions to be made [18].
42: Photochemical oxidation is contingent on carbon monoxide (CO), sulfur dioxide (SO2), nitrogen oxide (NO), ammonium, and non-CH4 volatile organic compounds [1]. The critical factor for this impact in both scenarios was CH4 emission from enteric fermentation, and manure management, and food production for cattle (Fig. 5). Furthermore, this impact category is predominantly influenced by the volatile organic

compounds (VOCs) of NOx and CH4, mainly from the manure of dairy cows, including nitrogen oxides from the use of fossil fuel for food transportation, and CH4 from the enteric fermentation process [10].

The AD scenario presented almost 7 % and 29 % lower photo- chemical oxidation than NAD1 and NAD2, respectively. Photochemical oxidation was the impact in which the AD and NAD1 scenarios presented the slightest difference. In addition, biogas had negative interference in the AD scenario. The operation of anaerobic digester plants, with biogas production, generates pollutants like CO2, CH4, phosphate (PO and SO2 Esteves [15]. That may have influenced our results for the AD scenario.

Furthermore, Battini [7] assessing the anaerobic digestion in dairy farms to the production of electricity, found that its manner is envi- ronmentally friendly and reduces environmental damage, although local impacts may increase in the form of photochemical oxidation. The au- thors describe that the reduced emissions of CH4 from storage and the credits gained from substituted electricity cannot compensate for NOx emission growth in biogas engine combustion. This may explain the slight difference between the AD and NAD1 results.

44: It is interesting to note the influence of animal productivity on environmental impacts. Table 3 shows that the values for GHG emissions from the studied farms are higher than the average values of emissions per cow in Brazil presented by the Ministério da Ciência e Tecnologia da Ciência and e Tecnologia [40]. This is because of the higher productivity per cow of the Embrapa and Colinas Farms. A higher level of produc- tivity per animal can reduce the overall GHG emissions and related impacts to milk production Seó [53], once the impact should be compared through the FU. When dividing enteric emissions per one kilogram of fat and protein-corrected milk, the UF, our result is lower than that shown by the MCT (2010) (Table 3).

Indeed, in Brazil, the productivity per animal is not as efficient as in other countries FAOSTAT Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations. Statistics Division. Livestock Primary. Roma, Italy [17], even where the breeding system is intensive, because of a lack of adequate management[44]. Brazil has productivity-five times lower than Israel and the United States, the first two in terms of milk pro- ductivity per animal[11],FAOSTAT - Food and Agriculture Organiza- tion of the United Nations. Statistics Division. Livestock Primary. Roma, Italy [17]. Increasing productivity per animal through changes in the breeding system is necessary to reduce the environmental impacts of dairy farming[44] The optimization of concentrated foods, seeking the dosage according to milk production, can improve environmental per- formance for the semi-intensive system. If associated with high milk production, milking frequency generates economic advantages for farmers, not requiring spending, although feeding is the key to boost milk production Bacenetti [5]. In this paper, we have observed that increased milking frequency, coupled with the use of anaerobic diges- tion reduces the environmental impacts.

47: The sensitivity analysis allows us to assess the effect of cattle feed on the final results. In order to do so, four distinct scenarios were built for AD, NAD1, and NAD2. The changes in the cow feed were evaluated keeping constant the regular milk production. Reducing concentrate consumption, scenarios S1 and S2 can promote improvements for all the six impact categories in different ranges, as shown in Fig. 6.

Within the distinct scenarios analyzed, the most prominent in AD was S2 (5 % reduction of the concentrate consumption), especially for the acidification potential, energy use, ozone depletion, and eutrophi- cation categories, with the potential to minimize the impacts by 4 %. Both scenarios S1 and S2 (2 and 5 % reduction of the concentrate con- sumption, respectively) in NAD1 can promote improvements for most of the impact categories. Mainly for eutrophication with the potential to minimize the impacts by 2 % and 4 % for S1 and S2 scenarios, respec- tively. In NAD2, the results were similar to the NAD1 promoting im- provements for most of the impact categories. S2 was the most important for eutrophication and photochemical oxidation with the potential to minimize the impacts by %. During the cultivation of the concentrate components, like maize and soybean, the most important environmental aspects for eutrophication are generated [3], which jus- tifies our results.

The least significant impact categories for S1 and S2 scenarios for both studied farms were climate change and photochemical oxidation once these impacts are mainly changed by CH4 emissions due to enteric fermentation and manure management (Fig. 3). Enteric methane is an important GHG that causes impacts on climate change and photo- chemical oxidation categories (Gonzáles-García et al., 2013). As cattle food has less influence in these categories, S2 showed potential to minimize the impacts by 1 % for climate change and 2 % for photo- chemical oxidation, while the S1 has not changed. The rate in enteric CH4 emissions from dairy cows is an effect of intake and composition of diet components that are fermentable carbohydrate, fiber, fat, and digestible energy intake Grandl [22]. Due to concentrate reduction, the cow probably eats more pasture to compensate for the daily food volume required, which justifies the analysis of scenarios 3 and 4. Then, when combined the reduction of concentrate with the digestibility of 55 (S3 and S4 scenarios), in both AD, NAD1, and NAD2, the results showed the impacts observed in climate change and photochemical oxidation cat- egories increase. In contrast, other impact categories remain with the same results presented by scenarios S1 and S2.

Corroborating with our results, De Léis [12], found that the confined feedlot (that has the most feed digestibility) system causes less damage to the environment in terms of the potential of climate change those the semi-confined feedlot and pasture systems (that have less feed di- gestibility). Therefore, considering that the digestibility of the food will not be reduced (and keeping constant the regular milk production), the results showed that reducing volume of concentrate consumption could create environmental benefits for all impact categories. Another alter- native to minimize the impacts of cattle food is to improve the nutri- tional quality of food, providing those with higher taxes of digestibility, which can bring environmental benefits and reduce the impact of LCA of milk.
